description: Claimed unbroken descent from Baal Shem Tov through Schneerson or related Hasidic families without generation-by-generation documentary proof.
telltale_signs: Sources only Geni, MyHeritage, Ancestry public trees, or copied GEDCOMs
No ketubah, birth, marriage, burial, revision-list, census, or contemporary rabbinical source for bridge generations
Generation gap inconsistent with known Hasidic chronology
Honorific titles used as identity proof instead of evidence
detection_rule: If claim_path contains {Baal Shem Tov, Besht} AND family_name contains {Schneerson, Shneerson, Schneersohn, Lubavitch} AND no source.type in {primary_record, archival_record, cemetery_inscription, contemporary_rabbinical_record}, flag high-severity fantasy.
propagation_vector: Geni merge-cascade and copied MyHeritage Smart Matches
real_alternative: Often the family is connected through discipleship, marriage network, same town, naming tradition, or collateral same-name relatives rather than direct descent.

description: Claim of biological descent from a Lubavitch Rebbe where the claimed line ignores known childless or collateral-only branches.
telltale_signs: Claim says 'descendant of the Rebbe' without naming the exact Rebbe
Movement affiliation treated as bloodline evidence
No documented parent-child chain
Known historical constraints of the dynasty are ignored
detection_rule: If claim contains {Lubavitch Rebbe, Chabad Rebbe, Schneerson dynasty descendant} AND exact ancestor_identity_id is missing OR no documented parent-child chain exists, flag high-severity fantasy.
propagation_vector: Community memory converted into biological descent in online trees
real_alternative: The family may have religious affiliation, service, marriage ties, or naming tradition connected to Chabad rather than direct descent.

description: Generic bridge into Twersky/Chernobyl dynasty through an unnamed daughter or son-in-law.
telltale_signs: Bridge person named only as 'daughter of Rabbi X'
No spouse name, residence, or date-specific evidence
Different trees attach incompatible children to the same rabbi
Dates allow impossible childbearing or marriage age
detection_rule: If parent.family_name in {Twersky, Twerski, Chernobyl} AND child.name is unknown_or_generic AND relationship has no primary citation, flag fantasy_bridge.
propagation_vector: Dynasty-prestige attachment in collaborative trees
real_alternative: Ancestor may belong to a local rabbinic family or in-law network without direct descent from the central Twersky line.

description: Claimed descent from Heschel/Apta line based on title similarity, rabbinical occupation, or town association.
telltale_signs: Record says rabbi, dayan, maggid, or rav but not exact dynasty
Apta/Opatow locality used as proof of descent
No chain through named children
Different men with similar titles collapsed into one famous rabbi
detection_rule: If ancestor.title contains rabbinical_title AND place overlaps known_dynasty_center AND direct descent is asserted with no child-level source, flag unsupported_rabbinical_claim.
propagation_vector: Place-title confusion in Geni and MyHeritage profiles
real_alternative: The ancestor may have served in the same region or community but not be biologically descended from the famous Heschel/Apta branch.

description: Connection to Schick/Maharam Schick line created from surname similarity and rabbinic title only.
telltale_signs: Surname Schick/Shik/Shick appears without documented parentage
Rabbi title added in unsourced online trees
Children assigned from incompatible regions
No yahrzeit, tombstone, responsa, or community-register evidence
detection_rule: If surname in {Schick, Shik, Shick} AND famous_rabbi_connection exists AND source_set contains only online_tree sources, flag medium-high severity.
propagation_vector: Name collision plus rabbinical-title inflation
real_alternative: Could be a separate Schick/Shik family or non-rabbinical collateral branch.

description: Claimed descent from Soloveitchik/Brisk rabbinical family through undocumented Eastern European bridge generations.
telltale_signs: Soloveitchik/Soloveichik/Soloveichik appears only as surname variant
Bridge generation lacks birth or marriage place
Claim relies on family story without records
DNA match is distant and in an endogamous Ashkenazi cluster
detection_rule: If direct descent to Soloveitchik/Brisk line is asserted AND evidence_score < 0.65 AND no primary bridge source exists, flag rabbinical_high_risk.
propagation_vector: Famous-rabbi attachment and Ashkenazi endogamy overinterpretation
real_alternative: There may be a surname or collateral community connection, but not necessarily direct descent from the famous Brisk branch.

description: Shortcut from a modern Ashkenazi family to Maharal of Prague through unsupported 17th-18th century generations.
telltale_signs: Long pre-1800 chain has no contemporary documents
Bridge contains repeated unknown spouses
Same chain appears identically in many public trees
Dates copied without places or source citations
detection_rule: If target_famous_ancestor in {Maharal, Judah Loew, Yehuda Loew} AND bridge_century in {1600s, 1700s} AND primary_source_count == 0, flag very-high severity.
propagation_vector: Copied medieval pedigree imported into public trees
real_alternative: Possible cultural or scholarly association, not proven biological descent.

description: Claimed unbroken descent from Isaac Luria, the ARI, through poorly documented Sephardic/Ashkenazi bridge generations.
telltale_signs: Mystical prestige ancestor appears as endpoint
No continuous civil or rabbinical evidence
Chain jumps between Ottoman, Polish, Lithuanian, or Russian records without migration proof
Calendar dates and death dates conflict
detection_rule: If famous_ancestor in {ARI, Isaac Luria, Yitzhak Luria} AND chain_length > 8 AND no contemporary source before 1800 exists, flag high-severity fantasy.
propagation_vector: Prestige genealogy and secondary-book copying
real_alternative: Family may carry a tradition of rabbinical learning without provable biological descent.

description: Rabbinical title or honorific treated as hereditary surname and used to connect unrelated people.
telltale_signs: HaRav, Gaon, Tzadik, Moreinu, Admor, Dayan, or Maggid appears in surname field
Different men with same title are merged
No patronymic or civil surname match
Children appear to inherit the title incorrectly
detection_rule: If name_token in rabbinical_titles appears in surname_field AND same-title merge connects unrelated places or families, flag title_confusion.
propagation_vector: GEDCOM name-field pollution and automated merge hints
real_alternative: Title should be stored separately as occupation, role, or honorific, not as identity or lineage proof.

description: A known student, disciple, follower, or Hasid of a rabbi is incorrectly converted into biological child or descendant.
telltale_signs: Source text says talmid, follower, chassid, disciple, or student
Online tree records the relationship as child or descendant
No birth or marriage document links the families
Spiritual lineage language becomes bloodline
detection_rule: If source_text contains {student, disciple, talmid, chassid, follower} AND relationship_type == child_of OR descendant_of, flag relationship_type_error.
propagation_vector: Misreading Hebrew/Yiddish rabbinical biographies
real_alternative: Record as spiritual association, teacher-student relationship, or community tie.

description: Two or more rabbis with the same given name, title, and town association are merged into one famous profile.
telltale_signs: Same title but different death places
Children's birth dates overlap impossibly
Different wives from different regions
Multiple yahrzeit dates exist
detection_rule: If rabbinical_profiles share title and given_name but have conflicting spouse, death_date, death_place, or child chronology, block merge and flag same_name_rabbi_conflict.
propagation_vector: Collaborative tree merge cascade
real_alternative: Maintain separate rabbinical profiles until a contemporary source proves identity.

description: A rabbinical book index or mention in a responsa is treated as proof of parent-child relationship.
telltale_signs: Source only mentions a name in an index
No explicit father, mother, spouse, or child relationship
Title or town is used to infer kinship
The same mention is copied to many profiles
detection_rule: If source.type in {book_index, responsa_mention, bibliography} AND relationship is inferred without explicit kinship terms, cap proof_status at hypothesis.
propagation_vector: Secondary-source overinterpretation
real_alternative: Use the mention as locality/occupation evidence, not parentage proof.

description: An unknown wife of a rabbi or notable man is upgraded to a daughter of a famous dynasty.
telltale_signs: Wife has no given name or only 'daughter of Rabbi X'
Marriage date is estimated
No ketubah, dowry, tombstone, or family book citation
The connection appears only in later online trees
detection_rule: If spouse.name is unknown_or_generic AND spouse.parent is famous_rabbi AND no marriage/source evidence exists, flag dynasty_daughter_invention.
propagation_vector: Prestige bridge inserted into missing female lines
real_alternative: Keep wife as unknown spouse with hypothesis notes until a marriage or contemporary source is found.

description: Family oral tradition of descent from a rabbi is promoted to confirmed status without documentary evidence.
telltale_signs: Source is oral history only
Exact connecting generations are unknown
Several famous rabbis are suggested interchangeably
No place/date continuity
detection_rule: If evidence_basis == oral_tradition_only AND claim_type == famous_rabbi_descent, set max_status = tradition_unproven.
propagation_vector: Oral tradition converted into GEDCOM parent-child links
real_alternative: Preserve as family tradition and search for bridge evidence.

description: Charlemagne descent asserted for a specific recent family using the population-level argument that many Europeans descend from him.
telltale_signs: No sourced gateway ancestor
Medieval links copied from public trees
No proof connecting immigrant, shtetl, or peasant ancestor to noble line
Argument relies on statistical plausibility rather than a documented chain
detection_rule: If target_ancestor == Charlemagne AND no documented_gateway_ancestor exists AND chain contains online_tree_only segment, flag high-severity unsupported royal descent.
propagation_vector: Royal gateway ancestor copying
real_alternative: Population-level descent may be statistically plausible, but genealogical proof requires a documented chain.

description: Claimed descent from King David through Rashi without complete, reliable, positive proof for each generation.
telltale_signs: Rashi appears as bridge to King David
Medieval-to-biblical segment has no contemporary evidence
Claim uses tradition as proof
Chain copied from famous lineage charts
detection_rule: If chain includes {King David, Davidic, Rashi} AND evidence before medieval period is not contemporary or primary-quality, flag extreme_claim_requires_manual_review.
propagation_vector: Prestige pedigree copying and unsourced rabbinical charts
real_alternative: Treat as family tradition or cultural claim unless supported by rigorous scholarly proof.

description: Generic Davidic descent attached to a Sephardic or exilarch-associated surname without family-specific evidence.
telltale_signs: Surname alone treated as proof
Prestige surnames such as Abarbanel, Dayan, Shaltiel, Nasi, or Abravanel used without local documentation
No direct source from the family branch
Multiple incompatible chains exist
detection_rule: If claim_type == Davidic_descent AND proof_basis == surname_only, flag high-severity unsupported claim.
propagation_vector: Surname prestige and online tree inheritance
real_alternative: Surname may indicate tradition, status, or later adoption but not proof of exact descent.

description: Automatic expansion from Rashi's daughters into every later Ashkenazi rabbinical family.
telltale_signs: A daughter of Rashi is used as universal gateway
Intermediate generations are vague or repeated
No local records for bridge into known 18th-19th century family
Chain appears identically across many online trees
detection_rule: If Rashi_descendant_claim == true AND bridge_to_modern_family has unknown_or_unsourced_generations > 2, flag high-severity shortcut.
propagation_vector: Medieval prestige tree imports
real_alternative: Keep Rashi line as unproven tradition unless each bridge generation is independently sourced.

description: Cohen/Levi status used to prove descent from a specific famous rabbinical or biblical figure.
telltale_signs: Cohen, Kagan, Katz, Levi, Levitan, Levitin, or Kogan used as proof alone
No geographic continuity
Y-DNA haplogroup misused as person-level proof
Multiple unrelated Cohen or Levi families merged
detection_rule: If surname_or_status in {Cohen, Kogan, Katz, Levi, Levitin} AND famous_descent_claim == true AND evidence_basis == status_only, flag unsupported.
propagation_vector: Priestly-status overinterpretation
real_alternative: Cohen/Levi status may support broad caste tradition but cannot identify a specific famous ancestor alone.

description: Shtetl, peasant, or urban artisan family attached to European nobility without conversion, adoption, migration, estate, or marriage records.
telltale_signs: Sudden jump from village records to noble manor lineage
No marriage, conversion, adoption, or estate record
Place names are similar but not identical
Noble surname variant added later
detection_rule: If social_status jumps from peasant/shtetl/artisan to nobility AND no transitional source exists, flag noble_bridge_gap.
propagation_vector: Same-surname noble-line attraction
real_alternative: Could be unrelated same surname, tenant relationship, estate residence, or patronymic coincidence.

description: Coat-of-arms or commercial heraldry page used as proof of noble or royal descent.
telltale_signs: Source is commercial heraldry or surname-history page
No individual-level documentary chain
Surname-only match
Different country, religion, or social estate
detection_rule: If source.category == heraldry_commercial AND relationship_claim == noble_descent, flag low-evidence claim.
propagation_vector: Commercial surname-history sites and copied profile notes
real_alternative: Use heraldry only as background context, never as proof of parent-child relationship.

description: A supposed gateway ancestor to royal or noble descent is inserted with one or more missing generations.
telltale_signs: Gateway ancestor has no baptism, marriage, probate, land, or court record linking to known parents
Generation count is compressed
Dates require childbearing at extreme ages
Same gateway appears in many trees with conflicting parents
detection_rule: If royal_descent_path contains gateway_ancestor AND gateway.parent_link.source_count == 0, flag gateway_unproven.
propagation_vector: Gateway ancestor copying from compiled pedigrees
real_alternative: Treat the gateway as unproven and verify with local records before extending medieval ancestry.

description: Biblical tribal identity or legendary ancestry is represented as exact genealogical parent-child descent.
telltale_signs: Claim connects modern person to biblical figure through legendary generations
No contemporary documentary chain
Tribal or community identity is used as genealogy
Dates span implausible gaps
detection_rule: If target_ancestor_period == biblical AND relationship_type == direct_descent AND chain contains non-historical or legendary generations, flag non-genealogical_claim.
propagation_vector: Mythic or religious tradition imported into family-tree software
real_alternative: Store as tradition, identity, or cultural note, not as evidence-based genealogical descent.

description: Ashkenazi DNA match forced into one close relationship path when multiple distant paths are likely.
telltale_signs: Many shared matches from multiple branches
Small segments across many chromosomes
Total cM appears higher than documented tree distance
Same surnames recur across several towns
detection_rule: If population_context == Ashkenazi AND match.total_cm < 80 AND shared_matches span multiple clusters, require multi-path hypothesis rather than single relationship.
propagation_vector: DNA match prediction copied directly into tree relationship
real_alternative: Model as endogamous multi-path relationship until triangulated segments and archive links identify the branch.

description: Acadian/French Canadian pedigree collapse makes DNA match appear closer than the documented relationship.
telltale_signs: Many repeated founding families
Multiple shared ancestral couples
High shared-match overlap
Relationship calculator gives broad or inconsistent estimates
detection_rule: If population_context in {Acadian, French_Canadian} AND multiple_shared_ancestor_couples >= 2, downweight single-path cousin prediction.
propagation_vector: DNA tool cousin estimate copied as exact genealogy
real_alternative: Calculate combined relatedness across all known paths.

description: Mennonite colony families from Prussia/Russia/Ukraine merged incorrectly due to repeated colony surnames and founder couples.
telltale_signs: Surnames Friesen, Wiens, Jantzen, Klassen, Penner, Reimer, Fast, Enns repeat heavily
Same first names recur in same colony
Colony migration dates are ignored
DNA match is endogamous and not branch-specific
detection_rule: If community == Mennonite AND surname in common_mennonite_surnames AND place is colony_region AND no colony register/source exists, flag colony_same_name_risk.
propagation_vector: Colony surname repetition and copied online trees
real_alternative: Use church books, colony registers, migration lists, and FAN-club evidence before merging.

description: Amish founder-population relatedness interpreted as one direct recent lineage.
telltale_signs: Common Amish surnames repeat
Many cousin paths exist
Marriage within closed community
DNA match assigned to wrong ancestral couple
detection_rule: If community == Amish AND ancestor_overlap_count > 1, require multi-path relatedness model.
propagation_vector: Single-path family-tree simplification
real_alternative: Represent multiple ancestral paths and avoid selecting one without segment triangulation.

description: LDS/Mormon pioneer families overconnected due to repeated polygamous family structures and shared pioneer surnames.
telltale_signs: Multiple spouses and large sibling groups
Children assigned to wrong mother
Same name in same settlement
Temple or ordinance data mistaken for civil proof
detection_rule: If community == Mormon_pioneer AND polygamous_family == true AND child_mother_source missing, flag maternal_assignment_risk.
propagation_vector: Shared tree merges and same-settlement duplication
real_alternative: Separate households by census, birth register, probate, and contemporary family records.

description: European royal pedigree collapse causes duplicate ancestors to appear as multiple independent people or creates ancestry cycles.
telltale_signs: Same medieval individual appears many times
Duplicate spouse or parent relationships
Impossible or circular ancestry
GEDCOM import repeats same noble profile under variant names
detection_rule: If medieval_royal_tree has duplicate_person_candidates with high similarity AND ancestry_cycle_detected == true, flag royal_loop.
propagation_vector: Large GEDCOM imports and duplicate medieval profiles
real_alternative: Collapse duplicate ancestors and maintain one sourced profile per historical individual.

description: Small DNA segments are overused to prove a specific 18th-century ancestor in an endogamous population.
telltale_signs: Segments under 7 cM used as decisive evidence
No triangulation
No archival connection
Many matches share the segment across multiple branches
detection_rule: If segment.length_cm < 7 AND population_context is endogamous AND no triangulation_group exists, do not allow proof_status above hypothesis.
propagation_vector: Chromosome-browser overinterpretation
real_alternative: Use small segments only as clues unless triangulated with documentary evidence.

description: Mennonite and Jewish endogamous clusters from the same Ukrainian/Russian Empire region are incorrectly merged through shared locality and distant DNA.
telltale_signs: Same Ekaterinoslav/Dnipropetrovsk locality appears in both lines
Mennonite colony surnames and Jewish surnames are combined without conversion or marriage record
Distant DNA matches assigned as direct ancestor evidence
Religious record sets are mixed
detection_rule: If community labels differ {Mennonite, Ashkenazi Jewish} AND link_basis in {locality_only, distant_DNA_only} AND no cross-religious marriage/conversion source exists, flag cross_endogamy_merge_risk.
propagation_vector: Locality overlap and distant DNA cluster overinterpretation
real_alternative: Keep as separate clusters unless a primary marriage, conversion, adoption, or household source proves connection.

description: Missing Holocaust-era records lead researchers to insert an invented bridge person between known survivors/victims and older shtetl families.
telltale_signs: Bridge person has no birth, marriage, death, address, or testimony record
Bridge person's only purpose is to connect two known clusters
Page of Testimony names are interpreted too broadly
Age and place are approximate
detection_rule: If bridge_person.source_count == 0 AND bridge links prewar_family to survivor_family AND Holocaust_gap == true, flag invented_bridge_risk.
propagation_vector: Reconstruction pressure after record loss
real_alternative: Represent as unknown parent or relative with hypothesis status and list required sources.

description: Two victims or survivors with the same name are merged based on a Page of Testimony or memorial record.
telltale_signs: Same given name and surname but different towns
Birth year range too broad
Submitter relationship differs
Spouse or parent names conflict
detection_rule: If Holocaust records share name but differ in town, spouse, parent, or birth_year by more than 5 years, block automatic merge.
propagation_vector: Holocaust memorial database name matching
real_alternative: Keep separate candidate profiles until multiple identifiers align.

description: Wartime alias, hiding name, camp spelling, or immigration name treated as original birth surname.
telltale_signs: Name appears first in postwar DP camp or immigration records
Prewar records use different surname
Alias has no parents attached
Different languages produce inconsistent spellings
detection_rule: If earliest_name_source_date > 1945 AND prewar_name_variant exists, classify as alias_candidate rather than birth_name.
propagation_vector: Postwar records imported as primary identity
real_alternative: Store alias separately and seek prewar civil, Jewish community, school, address, or residence records.

description: Yizkor book memorial lists are used to infer exact parent-child or sibling relationships without explicit relationship language.
telltale_signs: Several people listed under one town or surname
No explicit kinship statement
Same household assumed from memorial proximity
Ages not provided
detection_rule: If source.type == yizkor_book AND relationship is not explicitly stated, cap confidence at 0.45.
propagation_vector: Memorial-list proximity converted into family structure
real_alternative: Use Yizkor evidence as locality/surname clue, then confirm with civil, census, school, address, or testimony records.

description: DP camp or postwar residence is incorrectly stored as birthplace.
telltale_signs: Birthplace equals DP camp location
Birth date predates camp existence
Nationality and prewar residence differ
Record field actually says residence or registration place
detection_rule: If birth_date < 1945 AND birthplace in known_dp_camps AND source_field in {residence, registration_place}, flag birthplace_error.
propagation_vector: Field-mapping error in imports
real_alternative: Store DP camp as residence/event, not birthplace.

description: A person recorded as murdered is merged with a same-named survivor because names and birth years are similar.
telltale_signs: Death status conflicts with later immigration or census record
Different spouse or child names
Birth town differs
One record is memorial/testimony and one is postwar administrative
detection_rule: If one profile has death_event == Holocaust_victim AND another has postwar_life_event AND identity_score < 0.9, block merge and flag conflict.
propagation_vector: Automated hints and manual same-name merge
real_alternative: Treat as two candidates until conflict is resolved by parents, spouse, exact birth date, or testimony submitter.

description: Parents of child survivors or hidden children are reconstructed from surname and town only.
telltale_signs: Child has estimated parents with no record
Town has several families of same surname
Adoptive, foster, or hiding family confused with biological family
No prewar household evidence
detection_rule: If person.role == child_survivor AND parent_link.source_count == 0 AND basis in {surname_only, town_only}, flag high-risk reconstructed_parent.
propagation_vector: Postwar reconstruction and oral-history compression
real_alternative: Maintain biological, adoptive, foster, and guardian relationships as separate relationship types.

description: Women are merged incorrectly because Holocaust records list married name, maiden name, Hebrew name, or local civil name inconsistently.
telltale_signs: Same woman has different surnames across memorial and civil records
Children's records show another maiden name
Spouse names conflict
Birth town and residence town are treated as identical
detection_rule: If female Holocaust-era candidate profiles differ by surname and no marriage record links maiden/married name, require manual review before merge.
propagation_vector: Married-name indexing and memorial record ambiguity
real_alternative: Use marriage records, children's birth records, testimony submitter data, and cemetery inscriptions to resolve maiden name.

description: Russian, Ukrainian, or Belarusian patronymic such as Ivanovich is mistaken for hereditary surname.
telltale_signs: Surname field contains -ovich, -evich, -ovna, -evna in a record where separate surname exists
Father's given name matches patronymic root
Later records show a different hereditary surname
Gendered patronymic endings vary by person
detection_rule: If surname_field matches patronymic_pattern AND father.given_name root matches token AND region in {Russia, Ukraine, Belarus}, flag patronymic_as_surname.
propagation_vector: OCR/table parsing and Western GEDCOM import
real_alternative: Move patronymic to middle_name or patronymic field and preserve hereditary surname separately.

description: Jewish records using son/daughter of father or mother are parsed as surname or permanent middle name.
telltale_signs: Hebrew/Yiddish ben, bas, or bat construction appears in name field
Russian record gives patronymic but not surname
Mother's name appears where father is unknown
Same person has shifting patronymics across records
detection_rule: If source_language in {Hebrew, Yiddish, Russian} AND name contains {ben, bas, bat, son of, daughter of} AND surname is missing, flag relationship_phrase_not_surname.
propagation_vector: Literal translation into GEDCOM name fields
real_alternative: Store patronymic or matronymic as relationship evidence, not hereditary surname.

description: Polish, Belarusian, or Jewish -icz/-wicz surnames are incorrectly treated as non-hereditary patronymics.
telltale_signs: Established surname like Rabinowicz, Abramowicz, Dawidowicz, Markiewicz is split incorrectly
Same surname appears across multiple generations
Civil records use it as surname
Engine strips suffix and loses identity
detection_rule: If token ends with {icz, wicz, owicz, ewicz} AND appears as family surname across generations, do not classify as patronymic.
propagation_vector: Over-aggressive patronymic normalization
real_alternative: Treat as hereditary surname unless record context clearly indicates patronymic usage.

description: Female patronymic endings create duplicate profiles for the same woman.
telltale_signs: Same woman appears as Ivanovna, Iwanowna, Ioannovna, Janovna
Birth date, spouse, and children match
Surname is missing or variable
Different transliteration systems used
detection_rule: If female profiles differ only by patronymic transliteration and spouse/children/date/place match, propose duplicate_candidate but require review.
propagation_vector: Cyrillic-to-Latin transliteration variation
real_alternative: Normalize patronymic variants while retaining original forms as aliases.

description: Jewish double given names are mistaken for patronymics or fathers' names.
telltale_signs: Names like Zalman Moisei, Hersh Itzko, Rafail-Solomon split incorrectly
Second given name treated as father
Patronymic appears elsewhere with -ovich/-evich
Hebrew name and civil name mismatch
detection_rule: If second_name has no patronymic suffix AND known_double_given_name_pattern == true, do not infer father without explicit source phrase.
propagation_vector: Automated name parser overreach
real_alternative: Store double given names as given-name components and infer father only from explicit patronymic or record fields.

description: Archive table column shift places patronymic, surname, estate, residence, or notes into the wrong field.
telltale_signs: Surname column contains village or social estate
Patronymic appears in surname field for every row
Record has OCR alignment errors
Notes include years and words mixed into name fields
detection_rule: If parsed_row has high anomaly count across name/date/place columns, flag column_shift and require manual parse.
propagation_vector: OCR and spreadsheet conversion of metric books or revision lists
real_alternative: Re-parse from original image or structured transcription with column schema.

description: A true hereditary Slavic surname ending in -ovich/-evich is wrongly treated as temporary patronymic.
telltale_signs: Surname appears in same form across generations
Father's given name does not match the root
Women use feminine surname form separately from patronymic
Civil registers place the token in surname column
detection_rule: If -ovich/-evich token appears across at least two generations as surname AND father root mismatch exists, classify as hereditary_surname_candidate.
propagation_vector: Overcorrection by patronymic-detection rules
real_alternative: Preserve as surname and store patronymic separately.

description: Common Jewish male names such as Moshe, Itzik, Gersh, Yankel, or Zalman cause unrelated men to be merged.
telltale_signs: Same given name and surname but different patronymic
Two spouses overlap in time
Children born in distant towns at same time
Different cemetery or revision-list households
detection_rule: If common_name_score is high AND patronymic/place/spouse conflict exists, block automatic merge.
propagation_vector: Name-only hints and tree copying
real_alternative: Separate by patronymic, spouse, children, occupation, house number, and town.

description: Women with common names are merged because maiden name, married name, patronymic, and Hebrew name are confused.
telltale_signs: Same woman has different maiden surnames in children's records
Marriage record missing
Children assigned across two unrelated husbands
Hebrew name differs from civil name
detection_rule: If female profiles share given name but maiden_surname or spouse_set conflicts, require primary marriage or birth evidence before merge.
propagation_vector: Married-name imports and missing maiden names
real_alternative: Use child birth records and marriage records to resolve maiden surname.

description: Two people with same name in the same shtetl are merged despite belonging to different households.
telltale_signs: Revision list house or family number differs
Different father or patronymic
Different tax estate or occupation
Life events overlap impossibly
detection_rule: If same town and same name but household_number differs and patronymic differs, treat as separate unless a linking source exists.
propagation_vector: Shtetl name-density and weak indexing
real_alternative: Track household IDs and FAN-club associations.

description: People from different villages with same or similar place names are merged.
telltale_signs: Village name exists in multiple guberniyas, powiats, or counties
Gazetteer coordinates differ
Archive jurisdiction does not match
Religious community differs
detection_rule: If place_name has multiple gazetteer matches AND jurisdiction differs, block merge until place is disambiguated.
propagation_vector: Ambiguous village names in search results
real_alternative: Resolve by guberniya, uyezd, powiat, parish, synagogue district, and coordinates.

description: Passenger-list or naturalization age rounding causes the wrong immigrant to be attached to a European ancestor.
telltale_signs: Age differs by more than expected rounding
Nearest relative or destination contact conflicts
Town spelling is approximate
Several immigrants share the same name
detection_rule: If immigration_candidate_count > 1 AND age_variance > 5 OR contact_conflict == true, prevent automatic attachment.
propagation_vector: Immigration hint overattachment
real_alternative: Use chain migration contacts, naturalization papers, addresses, and sponsor names.

description: People buried in the same cemetery section are assumed to be direct relatives.
telltale_signs: Same landsmanshaft or burial society plot
No inscription naming parents or spouse
Common surname in plot
Burial society membership mistaken for family
detection_rule: If cemetery evidence is plot_proximity_only AND relationship asserted as parent/child/spouse, cap confidence at 0.4.
propagation_vector: Cemetery plot clustering overinterpretation
real_alternative: Use cemetery as locality/community clue, not direct kinship proof unless inscription or death certificate supports it.

description: Multiple GEDCOM imports create duplicate profiles that are then cross-merged incorrectly.
telltale_signs: Same person imported from several trees with slight date variants
Conflicting parent sets
Notes copied but sources missing
Media or source IDs lost in export
detection_rule: If duplicate candidates share name but parent_set_conflict == true, require source comparison before merge.
propagation_vector: GEDCOM import/export loss and duplicate merge suggestions
real_alternative: Run GEDCOM diff and preserve source provenance before merging.

description: A person with the same name as a famous historical figure is merged into that famous person's profile.
telltale_signs: Name and approximate century match but places differ
Occupation or title added after merge
Spouse and children are inconsistent with known biography
No evidence connects the local person to the famous individual
detection_rule: If candidate matches famous_person by name only AND place/spouse/children conflict exists, block merge and flag famous_name_attraction.
propagation_vector: Automated hints and prestige profile merges
real_alternative: Treat as same-name local person unless independent identity evidence exists.

description: Jewish-to-Christian conversion or baptism record is treated as ordinary birth record and linked to wrong Christian family.
telltale_signs: Adult baptism date
Godparents mistaken for biological parents
New Christian name replaces Hebrew or Yiddish name
Record contains conversion language
detection_rule: If record.type in {baptism, conversion} AND age_at_event > 10 AND godparent fields exist, do not infer biological parents from sponsors.
propagation_vector: Church-record parsing without religious-context rules
real_alternative: Store conversion as religious event; distinguish sponsors/godparents from biological parents.

description: Mennonite family transitioning to Lutheran, Reformed, or civil records is split into two unrelated families or merged incorrectly with Lutheran namesakes.
telltale_signs: Same surname and colony but different church books
Baptism practice differs by denomination
Civil registration starts after church records
German and Russian name forms alternate
detection_rule: If family appears in Mennonite colony records and later Lutheran/civil records with same household/place, classify as continuity_candidate rather than automatic duplicate.
propagation_vector: Denomination-specific database silos
real_alternative: Track denomination as event attribute over time.

description: Eastern Orthodox and Roman Catholic records in Polish-Ukrainian-Belarusian borderlands are mixed for same-name families.
telltale_signs: Rite or religion changes without marriage or conversion evidence
Polish/Ruthenian/Ukrainian name forms alternate
Parish jurisdiction differs
Children baptized in incompatible parishes on overlapping dates
detection_rule: If religion changes between Orthodox and Catholic AND no conversion/mixed_marriage evidence exists AND parish conflict exists, flag cross_rite_identity_risk.
propagation_vector: Partition-era borderland record aggregation
real_alternative: Use parish, rite, language, godparents, and village micro-location to separate families.

description: Hebrew/Yiddish Jewish name and Christian civil name are merged incorrectly because they sound similar.
telltale_signs: Moses/Moises/Moisey merged with Mikhail or Michael without evidence
Abram/Avraham merged with Adolf/Albert by sound only
No conversion or civil name-change source
Patronymic or parent names conflict
detection_rule: If name_equivalence is phonetic_only AND religion differs AND no conversion/name_change source exists, flag unsafe_name_equivalence.
propagation_vector: Cross-language name normalization too aggressive
real_alternative: Maintain alias candidates separately until confirmed by parents, spouse, occupation, or residence.

description: Burial in a cemetery with mixed sections or reused land is used to infer religion or family line incorrectly.
telltale_signs: Cemetery has separate religious sections
Burial register language differs from assumed family religion
Plot owner differs from deceased surname
Reinterment or memorial inscription involved
detection_rule: If cemetery.religious_affiliation is mixed OR section_unknown AND religion inference is based only on cemetery, flag low confidence.
propagation_vector: Cemetery database metadata overgeneralization
real_alternative: Use burial section, inscription language, death certificate, and congregation records.

description: Adoptive, foster, guardian, godparent, or sponsor relationship across religious communities is stored as biological parentage.
telltale_signs: Record says guardian, sponsor, godparent, foster, adopted, or raised by
Child religion differs from adult household
Biological parent names appear elsewhere
Age gap or household structure is unusual
detection_rule: If source_text contains guardian_or_sponsor_terms AND relationship_type == biological_parent, flag relationship_type_misclassification.
propagation_vector: Literal household-to-family conversion
real_alternative: Represent guardian/adoptive/foster/sponsor links as separate relationship types.

description: Children of mixed Jewish-Christian, Mennonite-Lutheran, or Orthodox-Catholic marriages are assigned to the wrong religious register or wrong parent line.
telltale_signs: Children appear in different religious registers
One parent absent from baptism or circumcision record
Surname and patronymic align but religion differs
Civil and religious records give different names
detection_rule: If parents have different religion labels AND children appear across multiple religious record sets, require event-level religion tracking before parent reassignment.
propagation_vector: Single-religion family model in tree software
real_alternative: Track each event by religion, rite, parish/congregation, and legal status.
